A tiny CLI that turns your shell history into a personal knowledge base.

Lumen explores the idea that your command history is a rough draft of your technical memory. It groups repeated commands, extracts useful patterns, and lets you annotate the ones you want to remember.

I built it as a small local-first tool because this kind of data should stay close to the person who created it.

Lightweight image labeling tool for small ML datasets.

FieldDeck is a focused labeling interface for small datasets. It is designed for the moment before a team needs a heavy annotation platform, when speed and clarity matter more than enterprise workflow features.

The app keeps the labeling loop tight: keyboard shortcuts, clear progress, and quick export formats for common machine learning pipelines.

An offline-first hiking companion with crowd-sourced trail conditions.

Trailmap is an offline-first map concept for planning routes and checking recent trail conditions. The project focuses on resilience: the core experience should still work when service is unreliable.

The interesting design challenge was deciding what information matters when someone is already outside. I kept the interface sparse, prioritizing route state, last-known condition reports, and notes that can be saved quickly.

Why token-driven design systems scale better than ad-hoc styling.

Design tokens turn repeated visual decisions into named choices. Instead of asking whether a color should be a little darker or a spacing value should be two pixels larger, the team chooses from a shared vocabulary.

The benefit is not only consistency. Tokens also make change less scary. If a product needs a denser layout or a softer visual tone, you can adjust the system instead of hunting through dozens of components.

A useful token system should stay small at first. Start with typography, spacing, color, radius, and shadow. Add more only when repetition proves that the system needs a new word.

A gentle on-ramp to Rust for folks who already love Python.

Rust felt intimidating to me until I stopped treating it like a faster Python and started treating it like a language with a different set of promises.

Python optimizes for expression and iteration. Rust optimizes for explicitness and confidence. The ownership model is the first real hill, but it starts to make sense when the data flow is small and visible.

My advice is to begin with command-line tools. Read a file, transform the contents, print a result. That kind of work makes borrowing and lifetimes feel concrete instead of abstract.

How breaking work into tiny pull requests changed how my team ships.

The biggest change I have made to my engineering workflow is reducing the size of each pull request. Smaller PRs make review faster, lower the emotional cost of changing direction, and create a cleaner history of why decisions happened.

A small PR is not just fewer lines of code. It is a complete, understandable unit of progress. Sometimes that means a refactor with no behavior change. Sometimes it means a tiny visible feature. The point is that the reviewer can hold the entire change in their head.

When I am planning a larger feature, I try to split it into setup, data model, interface, behavior, and polish. Each step should be safe to merge on its own.
